metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a reproducible verification step to the spike (story 95a5 AC#1): on a clean venv, install `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` and assert `tenacity` is present AND `from pydantic_ai.retries import AsyncTenacityTransport` succeeds; if the extra does not install tenacity, update the plan to add `tenacity>=8` as a direct dependency instead of relying on the extra.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` is a confirmed installable extra (and that `pydantic_ai.retries.AsyncTenacityTransport` ships from it), but does not reconcile this claim with the publicly-visible pydantic-ai package metadata that three prior plan reviews independently found to contain no `[retries]` extra. The plan has no reproducible evidence trail (e.g., which version added it, how to verify it on a clean install) so an implementer who hits an install failure cannot distinguish 'extra was dropped' from 'assertion was always wrong.' If `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` does not install tenacity, `pydantic_ai.retries.AsyncTenacityTransport` fails at import time, making the entire transport-retry mechanism — the epic's load-bearing core — inoperative.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Transport-retry dependency + injection point'; AC#8 ('pydantic-ai-slim[retries] is added as a dependency'); story 95a5 AC#1
findings.suggested_fix: In story 95a5 AC#1, change: 'pydantic-ai-slim[retries] + tenacity added to pyproject.toml' to explicitly include a clean-venv verification step. In the epic plan, add a fallback path statement: 'If pydantic-ai-slim[retries] does not install tenacity on a clean pip install (verify via pip show tenacity after install), add tenacity>=8 directly to the [agents] extra in pyproject.toml instead.' This makes the dependency strategy self-healing regardless of which assertion is correct.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` capability assertion before dependent child stories proceed: the spike (story 95a5) should explicitly confirm or deny that this extra exists in pydantic-ai v1.x; if it does not, update AC#8 and the plan to name the correct dependency (likely `tenacity` added directly to `pyproject.toml`'s `[agents]` extra), and rephrase the 'Architecture constraints' section from 'confirmed' to 'to be confirmed by spike'.
findings.finding: The plan's 'Architecture constraints & verified assumptions' section presents `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` as a *confirmed* extra that ships `pydantic_ai.retries.AsyncTenacityTransport` and `wait_retry_after`, citing verification against the installed package's `Provides-Extra` metadata — yet zero codebase-observable evidence exists (no import, no dep in pyproject.toml, no lock file, no package dump, no doc mention). The plan simultaneously delegates empirical confirmation to the spike (story 95a5), creating a logical conflict: a 'confirmed' assumption does not need a spike to confirm it. Multiple prior plan-review passes (cache entries 7468cc7, e3ed09a2) flag this extra as likely nonexistent in pydantic-ai v1.x, with pydantic-ai-slim's real extras being `[anthropic]`, `[openai]`, `[google]`, etc. If `[retries]` is not a real extra, `pip install pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` may silently no-op (pip ignores unknown extras by default in some configurations), leaving `tenacity` uninstalled and `AsyncTenacityTransport` unavailable at runtime.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Transport-retry dependency + injection point'; AC#8: 'pydantic-ai-slim[retries] is added as a dependency'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the 'confirmed' framing in 'Architecture constraints' to: 'the spike (story 95a5) will verify whether `[retries]` is a real pydantic-ai-slim extra; if not, `tenacity>=8` is added directly to the `[agents]` extra in pyproject.toml.' Update AC#8 to: '`tenacity` is added as a dependency (via `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` if the extra exists, else directly).' This makes the assumption falsifiable and avoids the logical contradiction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` is a real installable extra at v1.107.0 AND that `pydantic_ai.retries.AsyncTenacityTransport` + `wait_retry_after` exist at the claimed module path (e.g., `python -c 'from pydantic_ai.retries import AsyncTenacityTransport'`), and make the spike (story 95a5) the explicit gate for this verification — not just injection-point confirmation.
findings.finding: `pydantic_ai.retries.AsyncTenacityTransport` and `wait_retry_after` are claimed to ship with `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]`, but this specific module surface (`pydantic_ai.retries`) cannot be confirmed from any repo artifact. No import, no usage, no pip metadata file exists in the repository. The plan's own statement ('verified present in the installed package's Provides-Extra metadata') is an empirical claim that is unverifiable from the plan text alone — the [retries] extra may not exist at pydantic-ai v1.107.0, or the module path may differ.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Transport-retry dependency + injection point (evidence-backed)'; AC line: 'pydantic-ai-slim[retries] is added as a dependency'
findings.suggested_fix: Expand the spike (story 95a5) acceptance criteria to explicitly (a) run `pip show pydantic-ai-slim` and confirm `retries` appears in `Provides-Extra`, (b) import `from pydantic_ai.retries import AsyncTenacityTransport, wait_retry_after` and assert no ImportError, and (c) if the extra/module does not exist, document a fallback: use `tenacity` directly as a standalone dep with a custom `httpx.AsyncBaseTransport` subclass.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequence for the exit-2→exit-11 change (e.g., a transition period where exit 2 is still emitted with a deprecation warning, or a rollback procedure documented in the ADR).
findings.finding: The plan changes the exit code for systemic LLM failures from the existing INDETERMINATE exit 2 to a new exit 11, but provides no rollback path or expand-contract sequence for external consumers (CI pipelines, orchestrators, monitoring) that currently check for exit 2.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Exit-code signal' bullet; Acceptance Criteria — 'exit 11, unsigned'
findings.suggested_fix: In the exit-code ADR, add: (a) a list of known consumers of exit 2, (b) a deprecation/transition window or a compatibility flag (e.g., `--legacy-exit-codes`) if consumers cannot be updated atomically, and (c) explicit rollback instructions (re-pin to prior version + what to expect).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] The spike (story 95a5) must explicitly verify the full injection chain: that constructing `AnthropicProvider(http_client=<retrying-transport>)` causes the `AsyncAnthropic` client to use that transport for its HTTP calls, and that a synthetic 429→200 sequence retries at transport level (not at SDK exception level); document the verified constructor signature in the spike's acceptance criteria.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `AnthropicProvider.__init__` accepts `http_client=` and `anthropic_client=` constructor parameters at pydantic-ai 1.107.0 (stated as 'verified params: `api_key, base_url, anthropic_client, http_client`'), enabling a retrying httpx client to be injected below pydantic-ai's agent graph. The codebase only ever constructs `AnthropicProvider(base_url=_direct)` (runner.py:240); no `http_client=` usage exists anywhere. This is a load-bearing capability assertion: if `AnthropicProvider` does not forward a custom httpx transport to the underlying `AsyncAnthropic` client, retries will silently not fire at transport level (the Anthropic SDK processes the raw HTTP response before the transport can intercept the 429 status code and re-send).
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Transport-retry dependency + injection point (evidence-backed)': 'Injection is viable: AnthropicProvider.__init__ accepts http_client=/anthropic_client= (verified params at pydantic-ai 1.107.0)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/exit-codes.md` (with exit 11 entry and a note that exit 2 for INDETERMINATE is replaced) and `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_exit_codes.py` (updating the pinned table) to the explicit deliverables list of the gate-wiring story, and note the exit-code change in the epic's release-notes entry.
findings.finding: The plan introduces exit code 11 as a new 'block-but-retryable' signal for systemic LLM failures, replacing exit 2 (INDETERMINATE). However, `docs/exit-codes.md` is explicitly described as **frozen** since 2026-06-09 and is pinned by `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_exit_codes.py`. The current CLI (`_llm_commands.py:383`) returns exit 2 for INDETERMINATE. The plan's ADR mention covers the design decision, but the plan does not call out that `docs/exit-codes.md` must be amended, `test_exit_codes.py` must be updated, and any external consumers of exit 2 for INDETERMINATE (e.g., CI scripts, the Gerrit reviewer bot pipeline) will see a breaking contract change. Without this callout, the gate-wiring story's integrator role is incomplete and the CI exit-code gate will break.
findings.location: Architecture constraints — 'Exit-code signal: Exit 11 vs the existing INDETERMINATE exit 2 is bifurcated by the resolution_class'; AC#4 ('systemic → block-but-retryable (exit 11, unsigned)'); docs/exit-codes.md

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC to child 2eca requiring an audit of existing exit-code consumers (grep for exit-code checks in CI config, docs, and shell scripts) and a documented migration note with the exit-11 semantics.
findings.finding: No child enumerates existing exit-code consumers (CI pipelines, shell scripts, downstream tooling, documentation) and asserts they handle exit code 11. Exit 11 is a new one-way CLI-surface door; existing consumers that branch on 0/1/2 will silently mishandle a retryable failure as an unexpected exit.
findings.location: child 2eca (exit-code-11 contract)
findings.suggested_fix: In child 2eca's docs deliverable, include a 'migration guide' section listing the old exit codes and the new exit 11, and add a grep-based test that asserts no CI config file branches on only {0,1,2} without accounting for 11.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a plan-body paragraph (in Context, Architecture Constraints, or a dedicated section) describing what 'scope-vs-title breadth' means, why it warrants an ADR, and what it should record — so the AC item is verifiable.
findings.finding: The AC item 'ADRs are recorded for: ...the scope-vs-title breadth' references a 'scope-vs-title breadth' ADR that has no corresponding description anywhere in the plan body (Context, Architecture Constraints, or Success Criteria). The other three ADRs (exit-11, diagnostic-sanitization, deferred watchdog) are all described in Architecture Constraints, but 'scope-vs-title breadth' is not mentioned, explained, or motivated anywhere outside this single AC bullet. This is an orphan criterion — it measures something the plan never describes.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'ADRs are recorded for: ...scope-vs-title breadth'
findings.suggested_fix: In Architecture Constraints, add a sentence such as: 'A scope-vs-title ADR records the conscious decision that this epic's title ("LLM failure modes across the three gates") is narrower than its actual scope (which also covers output self-healing and silent-success detection), explaining the boundary and why out-of-title work was included.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define what the 'scope-vs-title breadth' ADR covers (e.g., the decision to expand the epic from 'rate-limit handling' to a full LLM failure taxonomy) so an implementor has a concrete topic and decision to record.
findings.finding: AC item 9 requires an ADR for 'scope-vs-title breadth', but this ADR topic has no definition, description, or body anywhere in the plan. The other three ADRs (exit-11, diagnostic-sanitization, deferred async watchdog) each have a corresponding description in the Architecture constraints or Success Criteria sections; 'scope-vs-title breadth' has none.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria item 9 — 'ADRs are recorded for: …the scope-vs-title breadth'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-sentence description in the Architecture constraints section, e.g.: 'Scope-vs-title breadth ADR: records the decision to deliver a full 8-class failure taxonomy and transport-retry overhaul under a title that implied only rate-limit/budget handling, and the rationale for not splitting the work.'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit async/sync compatibility check to the spike (story 95a5): demonstrate that `AsyncTenacityTransport` injected via `AnthropicProvider(http_client=...)` actually fires retries when `agent.run_sync()` is the caller (not just `agent.run()`), and document the finding in the spike's acceptance criteria.
findings.finding: The plan pairs an async transport (`AsyncTenacityTransport`) with `agent.run_sync()` — a synchronous call that internally manages its own event loop — but does not address the async/sync impedance. An async httpx transport requires an active async event loop when instantiated and used; `run_sync` creates a new event loop per call via `asyncio.run()`. Whether pydantic-ai's `AnthropicProvider` will correctly instantiate and pass an async httpx client transport through this internally-managed loop is unverified in the plan.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Synchronous runner' + 'Transport-retry dependency'; Context section — 'Self-healing retry at the httpx transport layer (AsyncTenacityTransport)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Designate one child (or the 2eca integrator) as the sole owner of httpx.AsyncClient construction at runner.py:238-245 and document that the combined client must carry both AsyncTenacityTransport and httpx.Timeout.
findings.finding: Children 95a5 and fd1d both configure the httpx.AsyncClient at runner.py:238-245 (95a5 sets AsyncTenacityTransport as the transport; fd1d sets httpx.Timeout as the read timeout) but neither child states who owns the httpx.AsyncClient construction or how the two configurations are merged into a single client. An independent merge of each child could silently overwrite the other's configuration.
findings.location: child 95a5 (runner.py:238-245, model-construction seam) and child fd1d (timeout wiring at model/agent construction)
findings.suggested_fix: Have child 95a5 own the AsyncClient factory function (e.g., `build_retrying_http_client(timeout: httpx.Timeout) -> httpx.AsyncClient`) that accepts the timeout from fd1d's config, so both configs are composed in one place rather than two separate construction paths.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enforce that child 95a5's spike integration test must pass (and be merged) before any other child in the {2,3,4,5} batch is merged, by adding an explicit dependency annotation to siblings that rely on the transport injection point.
findings.finding: The spike in child 95a5 is named as a prerequisite ('de-risk the load-bearing integration point before dependent work builds on it') but is placed in the same parallel merge batch {2,3,4,5} as children 30ff, fd1d, 978d, and 9a82. If the spike result is negative (injection fails, AsyncTenacityTransport doesn't forward correctly), those dependents' designs — especially 30ff's httpx-error classification and fd1d's timeout client construction — are invalidated, but no merge gate enforces spike-first ordering.
findings.location: child 95a5 (spike section) and merge order specification in parent and child 2eca
findings.suggested_fix: Promote 95a5 to merge step 1.5 (after a879 RED baseline, before the rest of the {2,3,4,5} batch) and add a gate that blocks 30ff, fd1d, 978d, and 9a82 until the spike AC is marked complete.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a detection mechanism in child 978d for schema-valid but semantically truncated verdicts (e.g., cross-check that required non-optional fields have plausible content, or use the existing finish_reason/stop_reason check to catch length-truncated completions before schema validation).
findings.finding: Child 978d's coverage of the 'empty/truncated-looks-complete' silent-success failure class is incomplete: the verdict schema-shape check validates only that the response is a parseable verdict object, but a truncated JSON response that happens to deserialize into a valid-but-stripped-down verdict would pass the check and be returned as a PASS/BLOCK. No child names a mechanism to detect truncation within a schema-valid verdict.
findings.location: child 978d (What: 'add a verdict schema-shape check (a parseable verdict object — NOT non-empty output'))
findings.suggested_fix: Supplement the schema-shape check with a finish_reason/stop_reason != 'end_turn' guard (already captured by check_stop_reason) AND a plausibility check on required verdict fields (e.g., if the gate is expected to evaluate N criteria but the verdict references zero, flag as suspicious truncation).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify in child 95a5's spike that reraise=True causes the original httpx exception (not a RetryError wrapper) to propagate after retry exhaustion, and add a test in child 30ff that the classifier correctly handles a post-retry-exhaustion httpx.ReadTimeout.
findings.finding: Child 95a5 specifies `reraise=True` for the Tenacity transport so exhausted retries re-raise the original exception. However, Tenacity's `reraise=True` re-raises the last attempt's exception. If Tenacity wraps the exception internally at any point (e.g., in a RetryError on some code paths), child 30ff's `except httpx.ConnectTimeout / httpx.ReadTimeout` branches would never match post-retry, causing those failures to fall through to NEEDS_INVESTIGATION instead of WAIT_AND_RETRY.
findings.location: child 95a5 (retry config: 'reraise=True') and child 30ff (mapping table: 'httpx.ConnectError/ConnectTimeout; httpx.ReadTimeout → RETRY_NOW/WAIT_AND_RETRY')
findings.suggested_fix: In the 95a5 spike's assertions, explicitly check the exception type that propagates after retry exhaustion. If a RetryError wrapper is observed, add unwrapping logic in 30ff's classifier (extract __cause__ from RetryError before matching).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default numerical values for Retry-After cap, per-request read timeout, per-tool timeout, and step caps in the Architecture constraints section (e.g., 'Retry-After capped at 60 s; read timeout 120 s; per-tool timeout 30 s; step cap 50') so all child stories use a single authoritative source.
findings.finding: The Retry-After cap value and the concrete numerical values for per-request read timeout, per-tool timeout, and step caps are never stated anywhere in the plan. These are defaultable gaps, but an executor building across 7 child stories will independently choose different numbers without a stated default, risking inter-story inconsistency and a spec that cannot be verified against a specific value.
findings.location: Architecture constraints ('generous per-request read timeout + per-tool timeout + step caps'); Acceptance Criteria bullet: 'Retry is honored and capped'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Default tuning constants' subsection under Architecture constraints listing: `RETRY_AFTER_CAP_S = 60`, `READ_TIMEOUT_S = 120`, `PER_TOOL_TIMEOUT_S = 30`, `STEP_CAP = 50` (or whatever the author intends), flagged as the single source of truth owned by the gate-wiring story.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Restate the spike AC as a concrete observable outcome, e.g. 'Story 95a5 CI passes and includes a test asserting that a mocked 429 → 200 retry sequence resolves without re-executing side-effecting tools, confirming the httpx-transport injection point works end-to-end.'
findings.finding: The Acceptance Criterion 'The transport-injection integration point is de-risked by a spike before dependent stories build on it' uses process/effort framing ('de-risked by a spike') rather than stating a specific observable outcome. It is unclear what artifact or test result constitutes 'de-risked' at the epic level — a passing CI run, a specific test name, a merged PR — making it not straightforwardly evaluable in-session against this AC alone.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, bullet: 'The transport-injection integration point is de-risked by a spike before dependent stories build on it; pydantic-ai-slim[retries] is added as a dependency.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'de-risked by a spike' with: 'Story 95a5 passes CI and its test log shows a 429→200 retry completing below the pydantic-ai agent layer (no tool re-execution), and `pydantic-ai-slim[retries]` appears in `pyproject.toml` dependencies.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the silent-success AC item (or add a dedicated AC bullet) to explicitly require build-time capability/config lints, specifying what they check (e.g., model capability flags, required config keys present) and how they are verified (e.g., a lint-time test or CI step).
findings.finding: The plan body describes 'build-time capability/config lints' as a deliverable under the silent-success layer ('build-time capability/config lints' in Context), but no AC item explicitly covers their presence, correctness, or verification. The AC for the silent-success layer covers only 'silent-success detection + the two output self-heal tweaks...and a degraded/INDETERMINATE verdict can never be signed', leaving the lints as uncovered work.
findings.location: Context — 'Silent-success layer'; Acceptance Criteria — silent-success AC item

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Standardize on one term throughout the plan: either 'resolution-disposition' (human-readable) consistently with a single defined Python identifier (e.g., `ResolutionClass` or `ResolutionDisposition`), and update all AC, Context, and Architecture Constraint references to match.
findings.finding: The plan uses 'resolution-disposition' (noun phrase, Context) and 'resolution_class' (identifier, Architecture Constraints and AC) interchangeably to refer to the same enum concept. While inferable from context, the dual terminology creates ambiguity about whether these are two distinct concepts or one — especially for the implementer naming the Python type.
findings.location: Context ('Classify what survives retries into a closed resolution-disposition set'); Architecture Constraints ('Exit 11 vs the existing INDETERMINATE exit 2 is bifurcated by the resolution_class carried on the verdict coverage'); Acceptance Criteria ('the classifier is total (unknown → NEEDS_INVESTIGATION)')
findings.suggested_fix: Choose `ResolutionClass` as the Python identifier (matching the existing `resolution_class` usage in Architecture Constraints) and use 'resolution class' as the prose term everywhere, replacing 'resolution-disposition set/enum' in Context and AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document a rollback/fallback path for the SDK `max_retries=0` change: either (a) keep SDK retries at their default until the spike is fully validated and transport retry is live, or (b) describe the explicit back-out procedure if the spike fails.
findings.finding: The plan sets SDK `max_retries=0`, removing the Anthropic SDK's existing default 2-retry behavior in favor of the new transport-layer retry. No rollback path is described for the case where the transport injection spike fails, leaving a window where the system has zero retries.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Transport-retry dependency + injection point'; Acceptance Criteria — 'Retry is owned at exactly one layer (the httpx transport), SDK max_retries=0'
findings.suggested_fix: Stage the change: ship `max_retries=0` only in the same story that validates and activates the transport retry, and include a one-line rollback instruction (revert to SDK default) in case the spike story is abandoned.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state whether verdict schema changes are additive-only or include removals/renames; if any breaking changes exist, add an expand-contract sequence or migration path for verdict JSON consumers.
findings.finding: The plan states that verdict schemas are modified by multiple child stories but does not specify whether those changes are purely additive or include removals/renames that break downstream consumers of the verdict JSON.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Shared-file integration' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bullet to the gate-wiring story's acceptance criteria: 'All verdict schema changes are additive (no field removals or renames); a schema migration guide is provided if any breaking change is unavoidable.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a documentation task to audit and update (or retire) existing references to `LLMUnavailableError`'s old opaque-collapse semantics — including runner.py comments, any operator runbooks, and CHANGELOG — so stale references don't strand users on the old mental model.
findings.finding: The plan replaces `LLMUnavailableError`'s current opaque-collapse behavior with a classified, actionable error taxonomy, but does not mention updating existing documentation or code references that describe the old error behavior.
findings.location: Context section — 'every LLM failure collapses into one opaque LLMUnavailableError at src/rebar/llm/runner.py:349-361'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate in the plan (or in the gate-wiring story's config section) which specific thresholds — per-request read timeout, per-tool timeout, step cap, Retry-After cap — are exposed as user-configurable keys in `_config_schema.py` vs internally fixed constants, and confirm none that are likely to change across deployments are hardcoded.
findings.finding: The plan references 'config keys' for per-request read timeout, per-tool timeout, step caps, and Retry-After cap but does not explicitly enumerate which thresholds are user-configurable in `config.py`/`_config_schema.py` versus internally fixed defaults. If any of these are hardcoded, they will be difficult to tune in production without a code change.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions / AC: 'Docs cover the taxonomy, the retry/timeout model… and the config keys (single source of truth, owned by the gate-wiring story)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a table or bullet list to the Architecture constraints or the gate-wiring child story listing each threshold, its default value, whether it is exposed in `_config_schema.py`, and the rationale for any that are intentionally fixed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all callers that catch or assert on `LLMUnavailableError` (CLI handlers, test fixtures, any downstream integrations) and add an explicit AC line or migration note confirming they are updated to the new exception/exit-code contract.
findings.finding: The plan replaces today's `LLMUnavailableError` at runner.py:349-361 with a richer failure-handling path but does not enumerate or acknowledge callers that currently catch or assert on `LLMUnavailableError` specifically (e.g., CLI error handlers, existing test assertions, any downstream integrators). If those callers expect the old exception type or the old exit-code contract, they will silently break or produce misleading behavior after this change.
findings.location: Context section ('collapses into one opaque `LLMUnavailableError` at `src/rebar/llm/runner.py:349-361`') and Architecture constraints ('Exit-code signal')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short 'Affected callers' table in the Architecture constraints section listing each site (e.g., `src/rebar/cli.py`, existing test files) and note whether `LLMUnavailableError` is retained as a subtype, renamed, or replaced — then confirm the gate-wiring story (the named integrator) owns those updates.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the diagnostic-sanitization contract in the plan (or a referenced child story): enumerate the patterns/categories to scrub (API keys, account IDs, Authorization header echoes), name the per-sink application points, and add a test that injects a synthetic secret-bearing provider error response and asserts the secret does not appear in any output sink (verdict file, session log, CLI stderr).
findings.finding: The plan expands the verbatim-diagnostic data path to new persistent sinks (verdict files, session log) but leaves the sanitization contract undeclared — no specification of WHAT patterns are scrubbed (e.g., bearer tokens echoed in Anthropic 401/403 bodies, account identifiers in rate-limit responses, Authorization header values), no per-sink contract, and no test requirement for the sanitizer itself.
findings.location: Architecture constraints & verified assumptions — 'Diagnostic sanitization' bullet; AC item 'ADRs are recorded for: the diagnostic-sanitization contract'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'DiagnosticSanitizer' spec to the gate-wiring child story (the named single-source-of-truth integrator): define a deny-list regex set covering `sk-ant-[A-Za-z0-9-]+`, Anthropic account/org UUID patterns, and `Authorization:` header echoes; apply it as a single call-site wrapper at the point the classifier constructs the `diagnostic` field; add a parametrized test asserting each pattern is redacted to `[REDACTED]` before the diagnostic reaches any sink.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a classification table to the plan (or a dedicated child story) mapping each concrete error signal (HTTP 429, 529, 5xx, 401/403, connection-timeout, UnretryableOutputError, etc.) to its resolution-disposition value, so the classifier has a fully-specified decision rule.
findings.finding: The 8-value resolution-disposition enum vocabulary is named but its classification rules (which HTTP status codes, exception types, or provider error signals map to which value) are never defined anywhere in the plan. The plan explicitly notes 'only the binary retry-or-not is behaviour-critical' but even the routing/messaging use requires a mapping an implementor can build against.
findings.location: Context section — 'Classify what survives retries' bullet; AC item 3
findings.suggested_fix: Add a table in the 'Settled design decisions' section, e.g.: 429/529 → WAIT_AND_RETRY; 500/502/503 → RETRY_NOW; 401/403 → CHANGE_SETTINGS; context-window → CHANGE_INPUT; UnretryableOutputError(refusal) → FIX_AGENT_DESIGN; unknown → NEEDS_INVESTIGATION. Identify which child story owns this as source-of-truth.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `resolution_class` as a named, enum-typed property to the relevant verdict schema(s) (or define it as a field on the `coverage` sub-object) and identify this schema edit in the gate-wiring child story's acceptance criteria.
findings.finding: The plan states exit-11 vs INDETERMINATE-exit-2 bifurcation is 'bifurcated by the `resolution_class` carried on the verdict `coverage`', but neither `plan_review_verdict.schema.json` nor `completion_verdict.schema.json` define a `resolution_class` field, and no child story is identified as owning the schema addition. The `coverage` field in both schemas is typed `additionalProperties: true` with no sub-field specification.
findings.location: Architecture constraints section — 'Exit-code signal' bullet; AC item 4; plan_review_verdict.schema.json coverage property; completion_verdict.schema.json
findings.suggested_fix: In plan_review_verdict.schema.json, add to the `coverage` object: `"resolution_class": {"type": "string", "enum": ["WAIT_AND_RETRY", "RETRY_NOW", ..., "NEEDS_INVESTIGATION"], "description": "Set on INDETERMINATE/systemic-failure verdicts; drives exit-11 vs exit-2 bifurcation."}`. Mirror the field in code_review_verdict and completion_verdict as appropriate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'faulty-output-in-reask' concretely: specify the triggering condition, what is appended to the reask prompt (e.g., the raw faulty output), and how this differs from or builds on the existing structured.py layer-4 bounded retry.
findings.finding: The plan names 'faulty-output-in-reask' as one of two additive output self-heal tweaks but never specifies what it means: the triggering condition, the reask message shape, and how it differs from the existing single bounded retry in `structured.py` (layer 4: 'a SINGLE bounded retry to the SAME model with the validation error fed back') are all absent.
findings.location: Context section — 'Silent-success layer' bullet: 'two additive output self-heal tweaks (schema-guided json-repair; faulty-output-in-reask)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either promote 2eca's verdict-schema edits to an earlier merge step (before the {2,3,4,5} batch) or introduce a stub schema that adds the optional fields early, so children can write and test resolution_class in coverage during their own delivery.
findings.finding: Child 978d produces `resolution_class = CHANGE_INPUT` (and other resolution classes) that must be written into `coverage.resolution_class`, but the verdict schema field `coverage.resolution_class` is added solely by child 2eca which merges last (step 6). During the parallel {2,3,4,5} merge batch, children writing resolution_class into coverage have no typed schema field to write into, making end-to-end integration untestable within the batch phase.
findings.location: child 978d (verdict-finalization path) and child 2eca (verdict schema owner, merge step 6)
findings.suggested_fix: Split 2eca's schema-edit work into a mini-step that lands alongside or just before the {2,3,4,5} batch, while keeping the gate-wiring and docs in step 6.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit merge prerequisite: child fd1d must be merged before child 978d, or 978d's hung-model AC cases must be gated behind fd1d's config keys being present.
findings.finding: Child 978d relies on child fd1d's per-request/tool timeout as the 'silent-hang backstop' for the silent-hang failure class but declares no explicit ordering prerequisite on fd1d. In the parallel {2,3,4,5} merge batch, 978d could merge before fd1d, leaving silent-hang detection without a backstop and the SC5 silent-success AC partially unfulfilled.
findings.location: child 978d (Scope/What: 'The per-request/tool timeout from story fd1d is the silent-hang backstop') and child fd1d
findings.suggested_fix: Add fd1d to 978d's 'Depends on' list and gate 978d's hung-model RED cases behind a feature flag / skip marker until fd1d's timeout keys are in the config schema.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC to child 2eca requiring an audit of all verdict-schema consumers to confirm they tolerate additive optional fields (or adjust the schema to remain backward-compatible).
findings.finding: Child 2eca adds optional `resolution_class` and `diagnostic` fields to all three verdict JSON schemas, but no child audits whether existing verdict-schema consumers use `additionalProperties: false` (strict mode), which would cause schema validation to reject the new fields and silently break consumers.
findings.location: child 2eca (verdict schema edits: src/rebar/schemas/*.schema.json)
findings.suggested_fix: Before merging 2eca's schema changes, grep for all verdict-schema consumers and confirm none uses additionalProperties: false without a corresponding schema update.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'MCP error shape' in child 2eca's description (or add a reference to the specification), or remove it if it is out of scope.
findings.finding: Child 2eca's What section mentions 'the MCP error shape' as a deliverable ('Per-class actionable messages; the MCP error shape; exit code 11') without defining what MCP means in this context or where the MCP error shape specification is maintained. No sibling defines it either.
findings.location: child 2eca (What section: 'Per-class actionable messages; the MCP error shape; exit code 11')
findings.suggested_fix: Clarify whether MCP refers to Model Context Protocol tooling; if so, add a brief schema for the MCP error response structure to child 2eca's What section.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
